_comment: v0.8 Tier-4 per-emulator base guides for additional emulators (9 titles). gameId=null means 'standard setup for this emulator, accurate for any game'. Download URLs point at official source/release pages.

steps.text: Download Citra MMJ (the performance-tuned 3DS fork) from its release page and install it.
No BIOS strictly required. Recommended settings.
Add your 3DS dumps (.3ds / .cia / .cxi) to a folder.
Select the game and play. Configure the dual-screen layout to taste.
Citra MMJ is optimized for performance on modest hardware compared to Azahar. Good for lower-end devices.

troubleshooting.fix: Some 3DS titles want shared-system-data dumped from a real 3DS — see the per-game guide.
Try lowering internal resolution from 4x to 2x or switching to OpenGL in the Renderer settings.
Disable async shader compilation and lower the resolution to 2x.

steps.text: Download Lime3DS (an active Citra fork) from its release page and install it.
No BIOS strictly required. Recommended settings.
Add your 3DS dumps (.3ds / .cia / .cxi) to a folder.
Select the game and play. Adjust the dual-screen layout as preferred.
Lime3DS is actively developed and compatible with most Citra game-specific guides. Good long-term choice.

troubleshooting.fix: Confirm the dump is a valid .3ds / .cia file; try a different game to rule out a bad dump.
Enable async shader compilation, or lower internal resolution to 2x.
Some titles need shared-system-data from a real 3DS — check the per-game guide.

steps.text: Download the latest Citron APK from its official release page and install it.
Switch keys (prod.keys) and firmware are required. These come from a Nintendo Switch you own — there's no download for them. Import them via Citron's key/firmware installer once you have them.
Install the recommended Turnip driver — Switch emulation is very GPU-driver-sensitive.
Recommended settings for a high-end Snapdragon / Gen 2.
Add your dumped games (.nsp / .xci) to a folder and set it as the games directory.
Install firmware + keys, add your game, and launch.
Citron uses a different shader cache strategy than Eden. First launch stutters while shaders build; the second run should be much smoother. Compatibility is still early-stage — not all games boot.

troubleshooting.fix: Re-dump prod.keys from your console and reinstall current firmware — mismatched/old keys are the #1 cause.

troubleshooting.symptom: Constant stutter even after shader cache
troubleshooting.fix: Lower resolution to 1x and confirm Turnip (not the system driver) is selected.
Citron is experimental — some titles are not yet compatible. Check community reports before assuming your setup is wrong.

steps.text: Download the latest Sudachi APK from its release page and install it.
Switch keys (prod.keys) and firmware are required. These come from a Nintendo Switch you own — there's no download for them. Import them via Sudachi's setup wizard once you have them.
Install the recommended Turnip driver — Switch emulation is very GPU-driver-sensitive.
Recommended settings for a high-end Snapdragon+.
Add your dumped games (.nsp / .xci) to a folder and set it as the games directory.
Install firmware + keys, add your game, and launch.
Sudachi is an older Yuzu fork — less actively maintained than Eden or Citron. Stable, but compatibility is narrower. Good fallback if newer emulators have issues with a specific title.

troubleshooting.fix: Confirm prod.keys and firmware are up to date. Sudachi may have lower compatibility than Eden — try Eden for that title.
Keep resolution at 1x, ensure Turnip driver is selected, and enable async shaders.
Sudachi may not support this game. Check the per-game guide or try a newer emulator like Eden.

steps.text: Install Dolphin MMJR2 (the performance-focused Dolphin fork) from its release page.
No BIOS needed. Recommended settings for high-end devices.
Add your GameCube/Wii games (.rvz / .iso). RVZ is the compressed modern format.
Pick the game and play. For Wii motion games, configure motion/IR controls first.
Dolphin MMJR2 trades some accuracy for speed — it's faster than vanilla Dolphin on demanding games. Expect higher FPS but occasional graphical quirks.

troubleshooting.fix: Set Shader Compilation to Hybrid Ubershaders in Graphics → Advanced.
MMJR2 is less accurate than vanilla Dolphin — if a game crashes, try vanilla Dolphin or lower the internal resolution.
Map the Wii Remote to touch/gyro in the controller settings before launching.

steps.text: Download the NetherSX2-Classic APK (unpatched build) and install it.
PS2 BIOS required (e.g. SCPH-70012.bin or your regional equivalent). Place it in the bios folder. This comes from a PS2 you own — there's no download here; place it once you have it.
On first launch, point NetherSX2 at your BIOS folder when prompted, then at your games folder.
Recommended settings for Snapdragon 8-class devices.
Put your PS2 games (.iso / .chd) in a folder and add it as a game directory.
NetherSX2-Classic is the unpatched build — less accurate than the patched version but sometimes runs games the patch breaks. For general use, prefer the patched version.

troubleshooting.fix: Wrong/missing BIOS, or the ISO is bad. Re-dump the BIOS and verify the game dump.
Classic is less patched than the patched version — try NetherSX2-patch if the issue is severe.
Drop internal resolution to 1x-2x, ensure Vulkan renderer, and keep speed hacks enabled.

steps.text: Download RPCSX (the experimental cross-PlayStation emulator) from its release page and install it.
PS3 firmware (PS3UPDAT.PUP) required for PS3 titles. Install via RPCSX's firmware installer. This comes from official Sony firmware — there's no download here; install it once you have it.
Install the recommended Turnip driver — PlayStation 3/4 emulation is extremely GPU-heavy.
Set expectations very low — RPCSX is bleeding-edge and most titles don't run yet.
Install your PS3/PS4 game (decrypted, from a console you own) into the emulator's game folder.

RPCSX is extremely early — it targets both PS3 and PS4, meaning resources are thin. Many games won't boot at all. Treat any working title as a bonus. aPS3e or a desktop PS3/PS4 emulator may be better for serious gaming.

troubleshooting.fix: RPCSX is in early development. Most games simply don't run yet — this is expected. Check the project's GitHub issues for known status.
RPCSX is not yet optimized for mobile. Keep resolution at 1x; accept that performance is severely limited.
Switch to a more mature emulator (aPS3e for PS3, or try on desktop RPCS3). RPCSX may simply not support this title.

steps.text: Download shadPS4 (the experimental PS4 emulator) from its release page and install it.

PS4 firmware / system files are needed, but setup is complex and varies by build. Follow the project's setup wiki carefully. These come from a PS4 you own — there's no download here, and shadPS4 is very new.
Install the recommended Turnip driver — PS4 emulation on mobile is demanding.
Set expectations extremely low — shadPS4 is very experimental and most titles don't run.
Install your dumped PS4 games (from a PS4 you own, decrypted) into the emulator's game folder.

shadPS4 is in very early development — it's the least mature PlayStation emulator on Android. Most commercial PS4 games will not boot. Use this only if you want to experiment with a bleeding-edge project.

troubleshooting.fix: shadPS4 setup is not yet user-friendly. Check the project's GitHub issues and wiki for the latest setup steps.
This is normal for most titles in shadPS4 — the emulator doesn't support them yet.

troubleshooting.symptom: Very low FPS or instant crash
troubleshooting.fix: Accept that shadPS4 is not yet viable for gaming. Revisit in a few months as development continues.

steps.text: Download the latest Winlator Frost APK (longjunyu2 build) and install it.
Install the recommended Turnip driver, then in Winlator go to Settings → Graphics Drivers → Install (.zip).
Create a new container with these settings (Containers tab → +).
Copy the game's folder (the one with the .exe) into the container's drive_c, e.g. Downloads, then point Winlator at the .exe.
Long-press the container → Run, browse to the game's .exe, and launch.
Winlator Frost (longjunyu2 fork) has good Wine 9.x stability and is a solid middle ground between vanilla Winlator and Cmod. First launch is slow — DXVK compiles shaders; let it sit a minute or two.

troubleshooting.fix: Switch DX wrapper to an older DXVK (2.3) or to WineD3D in the container's settings, then relaunch.
Set Box64 preset to Compatibility, and confirm the .exe is the actual game launcher, not a redistributable installer.
Lower the container screen size to 960x540, and make sure you selected the Turnip driver (not the system/llvmpipe one).
Install corefonts: container settings → Components → corefonts → install, then relaunch.
